sdo:itemDesc.dc:title: Handwritten letter from Hanslick to Schenker, dated February 15, 1894
sdo:itemDesc.dc:description: Hanslick is keen to discuss the idea of a history of melody with Schenker, but asks that it be postponed until after Easter.

tei:div.tei:p: Vielen Dank für Ihre freundlichen Zeilen und das Zeichen von Vertrauen, das dieselben enthalten! Ihre Idee einer „Geschichte der Melodie““Geschichte der Melodie”: For Hanslick, melody is the backbone of all musical compositions, as his well-known treatise, On the Musically Beautiful (1854), makes clear. “The motivation that is the point of departure for all of the composer’s creative activity is … the invention of a particular melody”; “Melody … is the ‘crux of the matter,’ the life, the first artistic shape in the realm of tone. Every additional determination, all realization of content, is linked to it” (Lee Rothfarb and Christoph Landerer, Eduard Hanslick’s On the Musically Beautiful: A New Translation [Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2018], pp. 46, 95). Accordingly, the history of melody would be of great interest to Hanslick. interessiert mich sehr, – oder richtiger: wird mich sehr interessieren, bis ich etwas freieren Geistes und Gemüthes sein werde. Ich bin jetzt sehr bedrückt durch den Tod BillrothsTheodor Billroth (1829–94), German surgeon, close friend of Johannes Brahms and of Eduard Hanslick, and influential in the world of Vienna music. und andere Freunde, dann durch schwere Erkrankung meines Bruders. Zu alledem überhäuft mit Arbeiten und inmitten der Conzertsaison! Sie würden mich also sehr verbinden, wenn Sie die mir versprochene Darlegung Ihres Planes bis zu OsterwocheIn 1894, Easter fell on March 25, hence Easter week was March 25–31. Since Schenker’s previous communication does not survive and Schenker’s diary does not begin until 1896, we cannot know precisely what Schenker’s “plans” were, but since his pivotal essay Der Geist der musikalischen Technik was published in May–June 1895 we might imagine that the theoretical issues that he wished to discuss with Hanslick related to that article. verschieben möchten, wo ich hoffentlich wieder aufnahmsfähig bin für ernste theoretische Gespräche.

Many thanks for your friendly lines and for the sign of trust that they contain! Your idea for a “History of Melody” interests me very much – or more accurately will interest me very much when I am in a somewhat more relaxed state of mind and disposition. I am now very depressed by Billroth’s death and that of other friends, further by the severe illness of my brother. On top of all of that, I am overwhelmed with work – and in the middle of the concert season! It would thus be most congenial of you if you would postpone the promised presentation of your plans to Easter week, when I will again hopefully be more receptive to serious theoretical conversations.
